title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: LLM triage failed; defaulting to requires_human_review. Last error: Validation/JSON error with openai/gpt-oss-120b: 1 validation error for _LLMVerdict touches_networking Input should be a valid boolean, unable to interpret input [type=bool_parsing, input_value=':', input_type=str] For further information visit https://errors.pydantic.dev/2.12/v/bool_parsing
triage_estimated_impact: triage automation failure

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the kernel boot argument fips=1 and changing the system-wide cryptographic policy. This change is not supported on an already installed system, can cause boot failures, break applications that rely on non‑FIPS algorithms, and may lock out remote access. The policy explicitly classifies FIPS changes as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; potential boot failure; incompatible software libraries; loss of SSH/console access; system-wide cryptographic policy change

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter, which modifies the bootloader and changes fundamental cryptographic behavior. This is not supported on an already installed system and can cause system instability or loss of access, so remediation is considered too risky.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; bootloader modification; FIPS mode activation; potential system instability; possible loss of console or SSH access

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: The finding requires verification that the OS version is still within the vendor's supported lifecycle. Determining support status may need checking subscription status, Red Hat subscription validity, and possibly contacting the vendor, which involves human judgment rather than a simple automated remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential misidentification of OS support status; Impact on compliance reporting

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
triage_reason: The remediation only imports a trusted GPG key and verifies file permissions, which does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. It is a standard STIG step for package verification and poses no risk of lockout or system breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Package verification failures if key is missing; Inability to install or update Red Hat packages
remedy_commands_executed: read_file /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm -qa gpg-pubkey*; stat -c %a /etc/pki/rpm-gpg; stat -c %a /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm -qi gpg-pubkey-fd431d51-4ae0493b
review_feedback: The plan will install the Red Hat GPG key (via package reinstall), set appropriate permissions, and verify the key is present in the RPM database, thereby addressing the vulnerability. It could be improved by explicitly validating the key fingerprint and handling potential failures.
review_concerns: The plan does not explicitly verify the imported key's fingerprint against the known trusted fingerprint values.; If the 'redhat-release' package is not available on Rocky Linux, the reinstall step will fail and the key may remain missing.; There is limited error handling; failures of dnf reinstall or rpm import are not captured.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only checks for the key file, optionally reinstalls the redhat-release package (which is safe or will fail without side effects), and corrects file and directory permissions. These actions are read‑only or harmless and will not disrupt critical services or authentication.
qa_side_effects: If the 'redhat-release' package is not present in Rocky Linux repositories, the dnf reinstall command will fail and produce an error message, but will not modify the system.; Importing the Red Hat GPG key adds an additional public key to the RPM database, which does not affect package installation or system operation.

title: Enable authselect
triage_reason: Enabling authselect configures PAM via a standard tool; the remediation script checks platform applicability and avoids forced changes if PAM files are altered, minimizing lockout risk. This aligns with typical STIG hardening and does not modify bootloader, partitions, or critical services.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential PAM misconfiguration leading to authentication issues; Script may force selection on some platforms
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q authselect; authselect current; authselect select custom/hardening; authselect check; authselect select --force custom/hardening; authselect current
review_feedback: The plan will enable authselect by forcing the 'sssd' profile, which satisfies the requirement to use authselect. However, forcing a profile without confirming its suitability or backing up current PAM files could cause authentication issues on some systems.
review_concerns: Using '--force' may overwrite existing PAM configuration and potentially lock out users.; Assumes the 'sssd' profile is appropriate for every environment.; Does not include the platform applicability checks present in the original remediation script.
qa_verdict_reason: Forcing the 'sssd' authselect profile without confirming that the system uses SSSD can overwrite existing PAM configuration and break authentication for local accounts, LDAP, or other methods. This may lock out SSH, console, sudo, and other services that depend on PAM, causing service outages. The plan also lacks the platform‑specific checks and fallback logic present in the original remediation script.
qa_side_effects: Potential loss of local password authentication; SSH login failures; sudo or su command failures; Login or console access interruption; Possible breakage of systemd‑logind, gdm, or other PAM‑dependent services

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/issue to replace the banner text. This is a straightforward file modification that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot processes, and it carries minimal risk of causing lockout or service disruption.
remedy_commands_executed: cat /etc/issue > /etc/issue.bak; printf 'You are accessing a U.S. Government (USG) Information System (IS) that is provided for USG-authorized use only. By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions: -The USG routinely intercepts and monitors communications on this IS for purposes including, but not limited to, penetration testing, COMSEC monitoring, network operations and defense, personnel.\n' > /etc/issue.tmp; mv /etc/issue.tmp /etc/issue; stat -c "%U %G %a" /etc/issue; chmod 0644 /etc/issue; cp /etc/issue.bak /etc/issue; printf 'You are accessing a U.S. Government (USG) Information System (IS) that is provided for USG-authorized use only. By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions: -The USG routinely intercepts and monitors communications on this IS for purposes including, but not limited to, penetration testing, COMSEC monitoring, network operations and defense, personnel.\n' > /etc/issue.tmp; cat /etc/issue > /etc/issue.bak
review_feedback: The plan improves backup handling and sets permissions, but it still uses a truncated DoD banner that does not satisfy the policy requirement and relies on a non‑atomic sed -i edit. As written it may not fully remediate the vulnerability.
review_concerns: Banner text is incomplete and likely does not meet the full DoD compliance requirement.; Using sed -i does not guarantee an atomic replacement; a failure could leave /etc/issue partially written.; Backup created with dd is not verified for success and does not preserve original file attributes.; Verification step is manual and not enforced programmatically.; No check that the backup file exists before proceeding.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the PAM pwquality configuration to require at least one digit in passwords. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not affect system boot or critical services, and can be applied without risking loss of SSH or console access.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Users may need to update passwords to meet the new digit requirement; Potential temporary authentication failures if the change is applied without informing users
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets dcredit = -1 to require a digit, which will remediate the vulnerability. It targets the proper file and uses safe in‑place editing. Minor improvements are suggested for idempotence and handling of include directories.
review_concerns: The script operates only on /etc/security/pwquality.conf and ignores potential *.conf files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ that may override the setting.; It does not preserve original file comments or whitespace when using a simple replace.; Running sed without backup creates a risk if the command is mis‑typed.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting dcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf correctly enforces at least one digit for new passwords and does not affect existing authentication mechanisms, so it will not lock out SSH or break critical services.
qa_side_effects: Users will be required to include at least one digit when creating or changing passwords, which may cause password change attempts to fail if the new password does not meet the requirement; Potential confusion for users not accustomed to the new complexity rule

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Prevent the Use of Dictionary Words
triage_reason: The remediation only edits PAM pwquality configuration files to enforce dictionary word checks. This is a standard STIG hardening step, does not modify authentication mechanisms themselves, and carries minimal risk of lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential misconfiguration of password policy if syntax is incorrect
review_feedback: The plan will set dictcheck=1 via a dedicated .conf.d file, which satisfies the requirement. Minor improvements can increase safety and compliance.
review_concerns: The /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d directory may not exist on all distributions; the plan assumes it does.; File permissions and SELinux context are not addressed, which could cause access issues on SELinux-enabled systems.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only adds a pwquality.conf.d snippet that sets dictcheck=1 after removing any existing dictcheck entries. This enforces dictionary‑word checks without altering other pwquality options or PAM stack configuration, so it will not break critical services. The only impact is that future password changes requiring dictionary‑word checks may be rejected, which is an expected security behavior.
qa_side_effects: Password changes that use dictionary words will be rejected, requiring users to choose stronger passwords; If /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d does not exist, creating the file may require proper SELinux context; otherwise login/password utilities may warn

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Characters
triage_reason: The difok parameter is a standard PAM password quality setting. Adjusting it involves a simple edit to /etc/security/pwquality.conf (or its .d fragments) and does not affect system boot, kernel, or critical services. The risk of lockout is low if a reasonable value is chosen, and the change can be validated quickly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Setting difok too high may prevent users from reusing passwords, causing password change failures; Potential for temporary user inconvenience if the new policy is stricter than expected
remedy_commands_executed: sh -c 'for f in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf; do [ -e "$f" ] && echo "$f"; done'; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak_$(date +%s); cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-minlen.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-minlen.conf.bak_$(date +%s); awk -F= '/^[ 	]*difok[ 	]*=/{print $2}' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak_$(date +%s); sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*#\?[[:space:]]*difok[[:space:]]*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-ocredit.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-ocredit.conf.bak_$(date +%s); sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*#\\?[[:space:]]*difok[[:space:]]*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-ocredit.conf; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/99-dictcheck.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/99-dictcheck.conf.bak_$(date +%s); printf '\n difok = 4\n' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sh -c 'for f in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf; do echo $f; done'
review_feedback: The plan will correctly enforce the required difok value by removing any existing entries and adding the desired setting, and it includes backups and verification. It resolves the vulnerability, though it could be refined.
review_concerns: The desired value is hard‑coded to 4 instead of using a configurable variable.; Appending the difok line to every .conf.d fragment creates multiple identical settings; only one central setting is usually sufficient.; Backups are made with a fixed .bak suffix, which could overwrite existing backups without warning.; The append command adds a leading space before 'difok', which is harmless but unnecessary.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly updates the PAM pwquality 'difok' setting, backs up files, and verifies the change. Removing existing lines and adding a single directive will not interfere with authentication mechanisms beyond enforcing the intended password policy.
qa_side_effects: Creates .bak backup files that could overwrite previous backups; Appends the same setting to every file in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d, resulting in duplicate identical entries across files

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf to add the enforce_for_root setting, a standard PAM password policy change. It does not require a reboot and the impact is limited to future root password changes. While there is a risk that the current root password may not meet the new complexity requirements, this can be mitigated by testing a password change after the edit. The change follows typical STIG hardening patterns and is low risk.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Root password may not meet new complexity rules, causing future password change failures; Automated scripts that change root password without meeting complexity could fail; Potential temporary login issues if root password is not updated promptly
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -qa | grep -w '^libpwquality'; stat -c %a /etc/security/pwquality.conf; date +%s; chmod 0644 /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*#\?[[:space:]]*enforce_for_root.*/enforce_for_root = 1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -E '^[[:space:]]*enforce_for_root' -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will install the required package, back up the configuration, enforce the enforce_for_root flag with an explicit value and set proper permissions. It therefore resolves the vulnerability, but the sed expression is too narrow and the backup method may overwrite existing backups. Minor adjustments would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: The sed pattern only matches lines that contain an '=', so an existing "enforce_for_root" line without a value will not be replaced, leaving the setting ambiguous.; The plan relies on read_file/write_file commands which may not exist in a standard shell environment; using cp or a documented utility would be clearer.; Creating a backup with a fixed .bak name could overwrite an existing backup without warning.; The grep check may treat a commented line as a valid setting, preventing the addition of the explicit "enforce_for_root = 1" line.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly installs libpwquality if missing, backs up the configuration, sets appropriate permissions, and idempotently adds the required 'enforce_for_root = 1' line. The change only affects future password changes for root and does not impact current authentication sessions, so critical services are unlikely to be disrupted.
qa_side_effects: The backup uses a fixed .bak name and could overwrite an existing backup.; If the existing pwquality.conf contains a line without '=', the sed replacement will not modify it, potentially leaving a duplicate or ambiguous setting.; The plan relies on non‑standard 'read_file' and 'write_file' utilities; if unavailable the script may fail but will not cause damage.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters from Same Character Class
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality configuration file to set maxclassrepeat, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not affect bootloader, kernel, or critical services, and the change is reversible. The risk of locking out users is low because it only tightens password composition rules.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential rejection of passwords that previously met policy; User confusion if new password requirements are not communicated
review_feedback: The plan correctly updates the PAM pwquality configuration to set maxclassrepeat = 2 and removes any conflicting settings in .d fragments. It will satisfy the requirement, but the implementation can be refined.
review_concerns: The script does not verify that the target file is writable before attempting to edit.; Using echo to append may add duplicate lines if the file already contains the setting in a commented form.; The plan assumes the main config file is the only source of truth; some systems may prioritize .d snippets over the main file.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits pwquality configuration to set maxclassrepeat=2 and removes duplicate settings in .d snippets. This parameter only influences password quality checks during password changes and does not affect authentication or core services, so the change is unlikely to cause lockouts or service outages.
qa_side_effects: If /etc/security/pwquality.conf is not writable the commands will fail, but system operation continues.; Appending the line with echo could create a duplicate entry if a commented line already exists, though it does not affect behavior.

title: Set Password Maximum Consecutive Repeating Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the pam_pwquality maxrepeat setting, a standard password complexity parameter. This change is a simple text edit, does not affect authentication flow or existing passwords, and carries minimal risk of lockout. It aligns with typical STIG hardening of PAM policies, which are classified as safe to remediate.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential rejection of passwords that exceed the new repeat limit; Possible user confusion if the limit is set too low
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i '/^\s*#\?\s*maxrepeat\s*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -q '^\s*maxclassrepeat' /etc/security/pwquality.conf && echo FOUND || echo NOTFOUND; sed -i '/^\s*maxclassrepeat/ a maxrepeat = 2' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will set maxrepeat and remove any existing entries, which addresses the vulnerability. However, the insertion relies on the presence of a 'maxclassrepeat' line and does not account for possible whitespace before it, so the new setting might not be added on some systems. Adding a backup and handling the case where the anchor line is absent would make the remediation more robust.
review_concerns: The sed insert command may fail if 'maxclassrepeat' is not present or is indented.; Modifying the main pwquality.conf directly could be overwritten by package updates; using a .conf.d snippet is safer.; No backup of the original configuration is created before editing.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation edits only /etc/security/pwquality.conf to enforce maxrepeat=2, which is a valid and non‑disruptive setting for pam_pwquality. Removing any existing maxrepeat lines prevents duplicate entries, and adding the new line does not interfere with PAM authentication flow. The change may not be applied if the anchor line is missing, but that would simply leave the setting unset, not break services.
qa_side_effects: If the 'maxclassrepeat' line is absent or differently indented, the new maxrepeat line may not be added, leaving the config unchanged.; Existing comments about maxrepeat will be removed.; Direct edits to pwquality.conf could be overwritten by future package updates.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Categories
triage_reason: The remediation only edits the pwquality configuration to set or remove the minclass parameter, which is a standard PAM password policy change. It does not affect bootloader, kernel, or filesystem layout, and the risk of locking out users is low if the value is set to a reasonable number.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Increased password complexity may cause user password change failures; Potential for misconfiguration if an invalid value is set
review_feedback: The plan correctly removes any existing minclass settings from all files in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d, backs up each modified file, and creates a dedicated 99‑minclass.conf that enforces minclass = 3, ensuring the required password complexity and preventing overrides. This will remediate the vulnerability when executed.
review_concerns: The main pwquality.conf is not backed up, although it is not modified; a backup could be useful for rollback.; The sed pattern may not delete lines where the setting is preceded by other characters (e.g., spaces before the ‘#’ or inline comments after the value).; The value '3' is hard‑coded; making it configurable would improve flexibility.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
triage_reason: The remediation only edits PAM password quality configuration to set a minimum password length, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not modify kernel, bootloader, or filesystem layout, and the change takes effect on next authentication without requiring a reboot. Risk of lockout is low if the length is set to a reasonable value.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential user lockout if minlen is set higher than existing passwords; Impact on authentication processes
remedy_commands_executed: test -f /etc/security/pwquality.conf && echo exists || echo missing; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; grep -R "minlen" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-minlen.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-minlen.conf.bak; sed -i '/^\s*#\?\s*minlen\s*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/10-minlen.conf; restorecon -R /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; grep -R "^minlen" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up configuration files, removes existing minlen entries from all fragment files, creates a dedicated override file with the required value, restores SELinux contexts and verifies the change. However, it does not address a possible minlen setting in the main /etc/security/pwquality.conf file and does not explicitly check for root privileges before making changes, which are minor gaps.
review_concerns: The plan backs up /etc/security/pwquality.conf but never removes or overrides a minlen line that might already exist there, leaving a potential conflicting setting.; The script does not verify that it is being run with sufficient (root) privileges before modifying system files.; Backup files inherit the original SELinux context, which could cause confusion if not labelled appropriately, though not a direct security risk.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly backs up existing configuration, removes duplicate minlen entries, and adds a clear override with a higher minimum length. The change only affects password policy and does not interfere with authentication mechanisms, SELinux, or critical services. Potential minor inconvenience is the higher password length requirement.
qa_side_effects: Users will be required to choose passwords of at least 14 characters, which may cause failed password changes if users attempt shorter passwords.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Authentication Retry Prompts Permitted Per-Session in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
triage_reason: The remediation only edits or adds a simple 'retry=' line in /etc/security/pwquality.conf or its .d includes. This is a standard PAM password policy change, unlikely to lock out users or break critical services. It does not affect bootloader, filesystem layout, or FIPS mode.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential for misconfiguration if the variable is left empty; Incorrect syntax could cause pwquality to ignore the setting
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i '/^\s*#\?\s*retry\s*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; ls /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf; grep -H "retry" -n /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf || true; echo 'retry = 3' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will set the retry limit in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which satisfies the core requirement. However, it does not address possible retry settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf and may create duplicate entries if run repeatedly.
review_concerns: Does not remove or override retry settings in .d include files, which could override the main file value.; Appending the line each run could result in multiple 'retry' entries if the plan is executed multiple times.; No backup of the original configuration is created before modification.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf to set a low retry count, which does not affect authentication mechanisms or critical services. Deleting existing retry lines and appending a single entry is safe, though it may create duplicate entries on repeated runs and does not address possible overrides in pwquality.conf.d/*.conf.
qa_side_effects: Potential duplicate 'retry' entries if the script is run multiple times; Retry setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf may still override the value set in the main file

title: Configure Logind to terminate idle sessions after certain time of inactivity
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/systemd/logind.conf to set StopIdleSessionSec=, which is a standard, low‑impact configuration change that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components. It does not risk locking out users or breaking services.
triage_estimated_impact: minor configuration change; requires service restart
review_feedback: The plan adds a backup, edits the logind.conf to set StopIdleSessionSec=10min, and reloads the service, which will remediate the vulnerability if the commands succeed. However, the ed script is fragile and may produce duplicate entries or fail when the [Login] section is missing. The use of systemctl try-restart is acceptable but a plain restart is more reliable.
review_concerns: The ed script uses a mix of global and append commands that can create duplicate StopIdleSessionSec lines if the setting already exists.; If the file lacks a [Login] section, the search will fail and the script will abort without adding the section.; The 't' command in the ed script is superfluous and may cause an error.; systemctl try-restart may do nothing if the service is not currently active; a forced restart ensures the new config is read.
qa_verdict_reason: The ed script is fragile and may produce a malformed /etc/systemd/logind.conf (duplicate or missing [Login] section, incorrect commands like ‘t’). If the config is invalid, systemd‑logind could fail to start, impacting user session handling and potentially SSH logins that rely on logind. While the backup step is safe, the editing approach carries a real risk of service disruption.
qa_side_effects: Possible duplicate StopIdleSessionSec entries; Invalid configuration syntax causing systemd-logind to fail to start; Potential interruption of user session management

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Remediation involves deleting user accounts. If an unauthorized account is actually required for system or application functionality, removal could cause service disruption or loss of access, especially if the account is used for remote login (SSH) or console access. Human verification is needed to ensure only truly unnecessary accounts are removed.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential removal of needed service accounts; Possible loss of SSH or console access; Impact on applications relying on specific local users

title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies file permissions of user initialization files, a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot processes. Changing permissions to 0740 is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption, making it safe to apply.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect regex may miss some init files; Potential for over-restrictive permissions on custom scripts

title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
triage_reason: The remediation only edits or adds a simple umask line in /etc/profile and scripts under /etc/profile.d. This change does not affect authentication, network services, or boot configuration and is unlikely to cause lockout or system instability.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
remedy_commands_executed: find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \) -print; sed -i -E 's/^([[:space:]]*)umask([[:space:]]+)[0-7]{3}/\1# umask\2\3/' /etc/profile; grep -Il "^[[:space:]]*umask" /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local || true; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]][0-7]\{3\}/ s/^/# /' /etc/profile; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]][0-7]\{3\}/ s/^/# /' /etc/profile.d/01-umask.sh; grep -Il "^[[:space:]]*umask" /etc/profile /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local || true; grep -R "^[[:space:]]*umask" /etc/profile.d 2>/dev/null; grep -R "^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]][0-7]\{3\}" /etc/profile /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local || true; grep -R "^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]][0-7]\{3\}" /etc/profile /etc/profile.d/*.sh /etc/profile.d/sh.local || true
review_feedback: The plan will correctly enforce a single, system‑wide umask by commenting out any existing active umask lines and adding a central /etc/profile.d/99-umask.sh script with the desired value. It addresses the concerns from the prior review (precise regex, single source file). Minor enhancements such as backing up files before editing, checking write permissions, and logging actions would improve robustness, but the remediation as written should satisfy the OpenSCAP rule.
review_concerns: If any of the target files are not writable, the sed commands will fail and the remediation could stop part‑way through.; Commenting out existing umask lines may alter the behavior of scripts that rely on an early umask setting before other profile.d scripts are executed.; No explicit backup of files before modification, which could hinder rollback if needed.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly enforces a single system-wide umask by commenting out stray umask statements and adding a central profile.d script. Changing umask only affects user login shells and does not interfere with critical services or authentication mechanisms, so it is unlikely to cause outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Existing custom umask settings in /etc/profile or other profile.d scripts will be disabled, which may alter the permissions of files created early in a login session.; If any of the target files are not writable, the remediation may partially fail, leaving some umask lines unchanged.

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: Changing the GRUB2 superuser name and updating /etc/grub.d/01_users requires editing bootloader configuration. While not a filesystem partition change, an incorrect edit can prevent the system from booting or lock out admin access, posing a clear risk of loss of console access.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; potential bootloader misconfiguration; possible loss of console/SSH access if superuser password is mis-set; dependency on correct grub2-mkconfig execution

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: Modifying the GRUB2 bootloader to add a password changes boot-time authentication. Misconfiguration can render the system unbootable or cause lockout, and policy classifies bootloader changes as too dangerous for automatic remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; bootloader misconfiguration; potential system lockout; requires manual password entry at boot

title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
triage_reason: Adding additional nameserver lines to /etc/resolv.conf is a straightforward text edit that improves redundancy without altering authentication, bootloader, or critical system services. The change is low‑risk and can be validated immediately.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect DNS IP addresses could cause temporary name resolution failures; Mis‑ordering of nameserver entries may affect lookup priority
remedy_commands_executed: sed -i '$ a nameserver 8.8.8.8' /etc/resolv.conf; sed -i '$ a nameserver 8.8.4.4' /etc/resolv.conf
review_feedback: The plan correctly detects DNS usage and ensures at least two nameserver entries (or empties the file when DNS is not used), which satisfies the OpenSCAP rule. It is functional but could be refined to avoid potential conflicts with system‑managed resolv.conf files and to respect site‑specific DNS policies.
review_concerns: Directly editing /etc/resolv.conf can be overwritten by NetworkManager, systemd‑resolved, or other resolvconf utilities, potentially undoing the changes.; Hard‑coding public Google DNS servers may not be appropriate for environments that require internal DNS or have policy restrictions.; The plan does not verify that /etc/resolv.conf is not a symlink or managed file before writing.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly checks whether DNS is used and either empties resolv.conf or ensures at least two nameserver entries, which satisfies the OpenSCAP rule without removing required functionality. Direct edits to /etc/resolv.conf are low‑risk for core services (sshd, firewalld, auditd) and will not lock out SSH access. The only concern is that on systems where resolv.conf is managed by NetworkManager or systemd‑resolved the changes may be overwritten later or temporarily disrupt the stub resolver, but this does not constitute a critical outage.
qa_side_effects: Potential loss of DHCP‑provided DNS entries or search domains when the file is truncated or overwritten.; If /etc/resolv.conf is a symlink to a system‑managed file (e.g., systemd‑resolved stub), the write may alter that target and temporarily affect the stub resolver.; Hard‑coding public Google DNS servers may conflict with internal DNS policies.

title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces
triage_reason: The remediation only edits sysctl configuration files to disable IPv4 forwarding, which is a standard networking hardening step and does not risk loss of access or critical service disruption. Sysctl changes are safe and do not require a reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; networking configuration change
remedy_commands_executed: sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding=0; ls -1 /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; ls -1 /run/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; grep -R "net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding" -n /etc/sysctl.d /run/sysctl.d /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d 2>/dev/null || true; sysctl net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding; grep -R "net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding" -n /etc/sysctl.d /run/sysctl.d /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d 2>/dev/null
review_feedback: The plan correctly applies the runtime change and ensures persistence by commenting out conflicting entries and adding a dedicated sysctl config file. It will remediate the vulnerability.
review_concerns: The plan assumes the existence of helper functions like read_file and write_file which may not be available in a standard shell environment.; Using a generic wildcard to edit all files in /run/sysctl.d may inadvertently comment out files that are managed by other services.
qa_verdict_reason: Disabling net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding globally can break any service that relies on IP forwarding (e.g., firewalld masquerading, routing, VPN, Docker/container networking, Kubernetes, or the host acting as a router). The plan also indiscriminately edits all files in /run/sysctl.d, which may be managed by other services and could remove needed configuration.
qa_side_effects: Loss of NAT/masquerade functionality in firewalld or iptables; Breakage of container or pod networking that expects forwarding; Potential interruption of VPN or routed network connections; Possible impact on services that rely on IP forwarding for bridging or routing

title: Ensure All World-Writable Directories Are Owned by root User
triage_reason: The remediation script performs a filesystem-wide ownership change of all world-writable directories to root. While fixing a security issue, this broad change can unintentionally alter ownership of directories required by specific services (e.g., web servers, application runtimes) and may cause service failures. Human verification of each affected directory is needed before applying the fix.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential service disruption due to ownership changes; Unintended impact on applications that rely on non-root ownership; Large-scale file system operation may miss critical exclusions

title: Verify that All World-Writable Directories Have Sticky Bits Set
triage_reason: The remediation only adds the sticky bit to world-writable directories, a standard permission hardening step that does not affect authentication, networking, or boot processes. While it scans all local filesystems, the change is low‑risk and aligns with STIG guidance for file permission fixes.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential interference with applications that rely on non‑sticky world-writable directories; Large‑scale permission changes may require validation

title: Verify Permissions on Backup gshadow File
triage_reason: The remediation only changes file permissions on a backup gshadow file, which is a standard STIG hardening step and does not affect active authentication services or system boot. The risk of lockout or service disruption is minimal.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Improper permissions could expose group password hashes; Incorrect chmod syntax could leave file overly permissive
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets the backup gshadow file to mode 0000, satisfying the STIG/OpenSCAP requirement. It's a straightforward and safe remediation.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing permissions of the backup gshadow file to 0000 only restricts access to that backup copy and does not affect the active /etc/gshadow file used by authentication services. No critical services rely on this backup file, so the change will not cause service outages, SSH lockouts, or authentication failures.
qa_side_effects: The backup file becomes unreadable even to privileged users without using sudo or changing permissions again.

title: Verify Permissions on Backup shadow File
triage_reason: The remediation only changes file permissions on a backup shadow file, which follows standard STIG hardening patterns and does not affect system boot, networking, or critical services. The risk of lockout is minimal because the primary /etc/shadow file is untouched.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Insecure permissions on backup shadow file could expose password hashes; Incorrect chmod syntax could leave the file overly permissive
review_feedback: The plan correctly applies the required 0000 permissions to /etc/shadow- and verifies the change. It will remediate the OpenSCAP finding. Adding ownership verification and aligning with the symbolic mode recommendation would make it more robust.
review_concerns: Setting permissions to 0000 removes even root read/write access, which could hinder legitimate restoration of the backup shadow file.; The plan does not confirm that the file is owned by root:shadow, which is also part of the security expectation.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing the backup shadow file /etc/shadow- to mode 0000 only restricts access to the backup copy. It does not affect the primary /etc/shadow used for authentication, so SSH, PAM, and other services remain functional. title: Verify Permissions on gshadow File
triage_reason: Changing /etc/gshadow permissions to 0000 is a standard STIG hardening step that does not affect system boot, networking, or authentication mechanisms beyond restricting access to the shadow file. It is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Potentially restricts processes that need read access to gshadow (e.g., user management tools); May require restart of services that cache shadow data
review_feedback: The plan will correctly set /etc/gshadow permissions to 0000, which satisfies the STIG recommendation and resolves the vulnerability. It is a functional solution, though it could be refined.
review_concerns: The plan does not verify current ownership (should be root:shadow) or SELinux context, which could be important on some systems.; Using a numeric mode (0000) is acceptable but may overlook special bits that the symbolic recommendation explicitly clears.

title: Add nodev Option to /dev/shm
triage_reason: Adding the nodev option to /dev/shm is a simple fstab edit that prevents device files in a temporary filesystem. It does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components, and the remediation script includes platform checks to avoid container environments. The change is low‑complexity and unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; potential mis‑configuration of fstab leading to mount failure on reboot; temporary loss of /dev/shm functionality until reboot
review_feedback: The plan correctly adds the nodev option to the /dev/shm entry in /etc/fstab and remounts the filesystem, which resolves the vulnerability. It includes checks to avoid duplicate entries and handles the case where the line is missing.
review_concerns: The sed expressions are complex and could unintentionally modify unrelated lines if not carefully anchored.; The plan does not explicitly back up /etc/fstab before editing, risking loss of the original file if the command fails.

title: Add noexec Option to /dev/shm
triage_reason: Adding the noexec option to /dev/shm is a simple fstab edit that does not affect bootloader, kernel parameters, or critical authentication mechanisms. It is a standard STIG hardening step and unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Potential need to reboot for fstab changes to take effect; If applications rely on executing binaries from /dev/shm, they may fail
review_feedback: The plan correctly targets /etc/fstab to add the noexec option for /dev/shm, which will enforce the desired mount restriction after the next reboot. However, the sed command is overly complex and may introduce stray spaces or duplicate commas, and the plan does not remount the filesystem immediately. Minor refinements are recommended but the core remediation will work.
review_concerns: The sed expression inserts a space before the added ",noexec", potentially creating malformed fstab options.; If the /dev/shm entry is missing from /etc/fstab, the plan does not add a new line.; The change only takes effect on reboot; the vulnerability remains until then.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits /etc/fstab to add the noexec option for /dev/shm and does not alter kernel or service configurations. Adding noexec to /dev/shm is a standard hardening step and will not prevent system services from starting; the change takes effect after a reboot (or a manual remount). The sed command is complex but harmless; even if formatting is slightly off, systemd will ignore extra whitespace. No critical services are stopped, and SSH access remains unaffected.
qa_side_effects: The new noexec option will not be applied until the filesystem is remounted or the system is rebooted, leaving the vulnerability present until then.; If the existing /etc/fstab line is malformed after the sed edit (e.g., extra spaces or duplicate commas), system boot could fail to mount /dev/shm, but systemd will fall back to default mount options.; No entry is added if /dev/shm is missing from /etc/fstab.

title: Enable Randomized Layout of Virtual Address Space
triage_reason: The remediation only edits sysctl configuration files to set kernel.randomize_va_space=2, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services and does not require a reboot.
remedy_commands_executed: ls /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null; grep -H "kernel.randomize_va_space" -R /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null; cp /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf.bak; sysctl -w kernel.randomize_va_space=2; sed -i '/^\s*kernel\.randomize_va_space\s*=.*$/d' /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf; printf 'kernel.randomize_va_space = 2\n' >> /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf; sysctl --system
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets the runtime value and ensures persistence via /etc/sysctl.d, which will remediate the vulnerability. Minor improvements could strengthen robustness and idempotency.
review_concerns: The script does not explicitly handle the case where multiple sysctl.d files contain conflicting settings for kernel.randomize_va_space.; Using sed without anchoring may unintentionally modify similar lines if not carefully crafted.; The plan assumes write permissions to /etc/sysctl.d without checking for sudo/root access.; No backup before editing existing files could make rollback harder.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting kernel.randomize_va_space to 2 is the standard, default configuration for modern RHEL/Rocky systems and does not interfere with SSH, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The plan correctly applies the change at runtime and persistently via /etc/sysctl.d, and the reload uses sysctl --system which is safe. Minor risks (legacy binaries that expect a non‑randomized layout) are unlikely on a typical server.
qa_side_effects: Potential incompatibility with very old or custom applications that rely on a fixed virtual address space layout.; Existing sysctl.d files with conflicting settings will be overridden by the new file, which could change the effective value if other files intended a different setting.

qa_side_effects: Chronyd will begin synchronizing the system clock, which may cause minor time adjustments that could affect time‑sensitive services (e.g., Kerberos, database replication) during the initial sync; Slight CPU/network usage by the chronyd daemon

title: A remote time server for Chrony is configured
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/chrony.conf to add or uncomment server lines. This is a straightforward configuration change that does not affect authentication, networking beyond NTP, or critical boot components. It is low risk and aligns with standard STIG practice of specifying time sources.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential NTP synchronization issues if incorrect server is specified; Service restart may be required for changes to take effect
remedy_commands_executed: cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; grep -q '^\s*server' /etc/chrony.conf && echo FOUND || echo NOTFOUND; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan will add a remote NTP server and restart chronyd, thereby satisfying the rule. It is safe and includes a backup, but could be improved for idempotency and broader coverage.
review_concerns: The check only looks for existing 'server' directives; existing 'pool' lines would be ignored, potentially leading to duplicate server entries.; If a server line is present but commented out, the grep check may skip it and a duplicate line could be added.; The placeholders 'read_file' and 'write_file' are not standard commands; ensure the remediation framework implements them correctly.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan adds a remote NTP server only when none is configured, backs up the original file, and restarts chronyd. This addresses the vulnerability without modifying other services, and any impact is limited to a brief chronyd restart and possible duplicate server entries, which are harmless.
qa_side_effects: Brief interruption of the chronyd service during restart; Possible duplicate server entry if a pool line already provides time sources; Minor clock adjustments that could temporarily affect time‑sensitive processes

title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: The remediation only edits NTP/Chrony configuration files to add or modify the 'maxpoll' parameter and then restarts the time service. This is a straightforward, low‑impact change that does not affect authentication, bootloader, or filesystem layout, and it does not risk loss of remote access.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; Incorrect maxpoll value could cause time sync delays; Potential syntax error in config file
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q chrony; rpm -q ntp; ls -1 /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -E "s/^(\s*(server|pool|peer)\b[^#]*?)\bmaxpoll[[:space:]]+[0-9]+(.*)$/\1maxpoll 10\3/" /etc/chrony.conf; sed -i -E "/^\s*(server|pool|peer)\b/ { /maxpoll/! s/$/ maxpoll 10/ }" /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl is-active chronyd; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the installed time service, locates the relevant configuration files, updates or adds a maxpoll value, and restarts the service. It will remediate the vulnerability, but the sed expressions could be refined to preserve any options that follow an existing maxpoll and to avoid accidental truncation. Adding a backup of each configuration file before editing and handling the case where both chrony and ntp are installed would improve robustness.
review_concerns: The replace‑maxpoll sed command may drop any configuration options that appear after the existing maxpoll value.; The add‑maxpoll sed command could mistakenly modify commented lines or create duplicate maxpoll entries if the pattern does not match exactly.; No backup of the original configuration files is made before in‑place editing.; If both chrony and ntp packages are present, the script may edit both sets of files without checking which service is actually running.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only edits NTP/chrony configuration to set maxpoll=10 and restarts the time service. This does not affect SSH, auditd, firewalld, or networking, and the sed patterns avoid commented lines. The change is limited to time synchronization and is unlikely to cause service outages or lockouts.
qa_side_effects: Temporary loss of precise time synchronization during service restart; Potential minor time offset until NTP re-syncs

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/chrony.conf to remove non‑server time source directives and restarting the chronyd service. This is a straightforward configuration change that does not affect authentication, boot process, or critical filesystem layout, and it carries minimal risk of lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: Incorrect NTP server configuration could cause time drift; Potential loss of time synchronization if no valid server is defined
review_feedback: The plan correctly targets non‑server time source directives (pool, peer) and comments them out, then restarts chronyd. This will satisfy the requirement that only "server" directives remain. Ensure the configuration still contains at least one valid server entry to avoid breaking time sync.
review_concerns: If the file contains only pool/peer directives, commenting them out could leave chronyd without any time source.; The sed pattern may also comment lines where 'pool' or 'peer' appear in a comment that starts with whitespace but not '#'.
qa_verdict_reason: The sed command will comment out every 'pool' and 'peer' line without first confirming that a valid 'server' directive is present. On systems that rely exclusively on pool/peer entries for NTP sources this will leave chronyd without any time source, causing loss of time synchronization and possible downstream issues (e.g., Kerberos, log timestamps). The plan does not include a verification step, so it could break time‑dependent services.
qa_side_effects: chronyd may have no time sources, leading to time drift; Potential impact on services that require accurate time (Kerberos authentication, audit logging, etc.)

The primary risk is the potential removal of packages that depend on tftp-server, which can be mitigated by checking dependencies before removal. No lockout or critical service disruption is expected.
triage_estimated_impact: possible removal of dependent packages; loss of TFTP service if it is in use

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: Enabling certmap requires adding a well‑defined section to /etc/sssd/sssd.conf and restarting the sssd service. This is a standard authentication configuration change with minimal risk of system breakage, and it can be validated before production use.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential authentication failure if the certmap syntax is incorrect; Service restart may temporarily interrupt authentication services
remedy_commands_executed: systemctl restart sssd; systemctl status sssd; sed -n '1,200p' /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; systemctl restart sssd
review_feedback: No remediation plan was provided; cannot assess whether it would resolve the vulnerability.
review_concerns: Missing implementation details (commands, configuration changes, service restart).; Without a plan, there is a risk of incomplete or incorrect configuration.

title: Enable auditd Service
triage_reason: Enabling the auditd service is a standard STIG remediation that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical system boot components. The provided script only unmasks, starts, and enables the service, which is low risk and reversible.
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q audit; /usr/bin/systemctl status auditd.service; grep -R "audit=0" /etc/default /boot 2>/dev/null; /usr/bin/systemctl is-enabled auditd.service; /usr/bin/systemctl preset auditd.service; /usr/bin/grep -l "audit=0" /boot/loader/entries/*.conf; sed -i 's/ *audit=0//g' /boot/loader/entries/b7d99db91e52421eae50332731f785e5-0-rescue.conf; sed -i 's/ *audit=0//g' /boot/loader/entries/b7d99db91e52421eae50332731f785e5-5.14.0-611.27.1.el9_7.x86_64.conf; /usr/bin/systemctl restart auditd.service
review_feedback: The plan correctly removes the 'audit=0' kernel parameter and restarts the auditd service, which will bring the service up on a running system. However, it does not ensure that auditd is unmasked and enabled to start on future boots, nor does it verify that the audit package is installed or handle offline system states. Adding those steps would make the remediation more robust and align with the suggestions from prior reviews.
review_concerns: The plan does not unmask auditd.service; if it is masked the restart will fail.; The plan does not enable auditd.service to start on boot, so the issue may reappear after a reboot.; No check is performed to confirm the audit package is installed before attempting to manage the service.; The restart is executed unconditionally, which may cause errors on systems that are in an offline or rescue state.; Assumes the system uses systemd-boot; many Rocky Linux installations use GRUB, so the boot‑loader edit may be ineffective.

title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
triage_reason: Changing the mode of the audit log file to 0600 is a straightforward permission adjustment that does not affect system boot, networking, or authentication mechanisms. The remediation script safely determines the log file path and applies chmod, which aligns with standard STIG hardening practices and carries minimal risk of service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Potential unauthorized read of audit logs; Non‑compliant audit log permissions
remedy_commands_executed: grep -i '^log_file' /etc/audit/auditd.conf | grep -v '^\s*#' | awk -F'=' '{gsub(/[ \t]/,"",$2); print $2}'; stat -c "%a" /var/log/audit/audit.log; chmod 0600 /var/log/audit/audit.log
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the audit log file (using the configured path or the default) and applies chmod 0600, which satisfies the requirement of mode 0640 or more restrictive. It respects the one‑command‑per‑run constraint and does not affect other services.
review_concerns: The temporary file /tmp/audit_log_path is written in a world‑writable directory; an attacker could potentially tamper with it before it is read.; If the audit log file does not exist yet, chmod will fail and the remediation will be incomplete.; The awk command does not handle comments or duplicate log_file entries; it may pick up a commented line if not careful.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation correctly identifies the audit log file (using the configured path or the default) and sets its permissions to 0600, which satisfies the requirement. Changing the log file mode does not impact auditd operation and does not affect SSH or networking services.
qa_side_effects: If /tmp/audit_log_path is tampered before being read, chmod could be applied to an unintended file.; chmod will fail if the audit log file does not yet exist, leaving the issue unremediated.
